posts.cooked:
WHITE BURGUNDIES AT ASIA GRAND - (14/4/2023)

  • 2014 Déhu Père & Fils Champagne Cuvée La Rue des Noyers Extra Brut - France, Champagne
    Nose a little funky but blew off with air. Acidity was quite prominent at first but put on weight with time in the glass. Fresh red fruit led me to a Pinot Noir-dominant blend, stylistically it was similar to Savart I felt. Rather surprised it was a Meunier since it didn’t have overt ripeness. (91 pts.)
  • 2017 Domaine / Maison Vincent Girardin Meursault Les Narvaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault
    Brought for a friend. Resolutely old-school white Burgundy with lush, creamy oak. Not my kind of style normally but thought this was well-made, the acidity ensured that the fatness wasn’t overbearing. (91 pts.)
  • 2007 Domaine Potinet-Ampeau Meursault 1er Cru Charmes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault 1er Cru
    Guessed 2005 Meursault Genevrieres, not the first time I conflated warmth of plot vs vintage. Rich, old-school Burgundy with high acid that didn’t come across as integrated. Was opened a couple hours prior to dinner so the sulphur wasn’t prominent. Youthful but won’t ever be a great wine, rather pedestrian for me. (89 pts.)
  • 2014 Benjamin Leroux Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Les Murgers des Dents de Chien Blanc - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Saint-Aubin 1er Cru
    Brought for a friend. Wow this turned out to be the favourite on the night for most, including myself. Reductive nose that got more floral with orchard fruit after time in the glass. Excellent tension and balance between both citrus and tropical fruit, as well as hazelnut and saline notes. Haven’t had many Leroux whites but based on this they are well worth seeking out. (92 pts.)
  • 2013 Domaine Henri Gouges Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru La Perrière Blanc - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru
    Nobody could place this as a CdN white unsurprisingly. Acid was a little screechy with sweet ripe fruit that suggested a little botrytis so I went for 2010 Puligny. Others liked it more than I did, thought it was interesting but not very enjoyable to me. Treat to have tried this though. (89 pts.)
  • 2020 Dureuil-Janthial Rully 1er Cru Le Meix Cadot Blanc - France, Burgundy, Côte Chalonnaise, Rully 1er Cru
    My contribution. Completely different from the 2015 that I tried previously, not sure if it is a change of style or bottle variation. Rather pale straw in colour, dominated by strict acidity and citrus fruit. The impression I had was that these wines had a layer of oak that lent it a certain seriousness and weight that were lacking here. Definitely not worth the current market price unfortunately.
    EDIT: On second day, became much richer as the oak became more prominent. A lot more serious in the style that I prefer, score upgraded.
    (92 pts.)
  • 2017 Domaine Jacques Carillon Puligny-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet
    Much more open than my previous experience. Pale greenish colour, quintessential Puligny from a more classic year. Pure, elegant citrus and green apple fruit with a very nice acidic spine. Not as much depth as the 2014 but maybe that will come with time. (91 pts.)
  • 2016 Domaine Patrick Javillier Meursault Tête de Murgers Cuvée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault
    Surprised at this showing despite it being from a weaker year. Would have gone new-world on this if I was completely bilinded. Exotic, creamy fruit that had an overt sweetness that I dislike in Chardonnays. Severely lacking in acidity. (85 pts.)
  • 2014 Domaine Pattes Loup Chablis 1er Cru Butteaux - France, Burgundy, Chablis, Chablis 1er Cru
    Had lots of variability with his wine unfortunately. A little dark in colour, palate was definitely a little advanced for this. Drinkable but not very interesting.
  • 2015 Domaine Thibert Père et Fils Pouilly-Fuissé Les Ménétrières - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Pouilly-Fuissé
    Previous experience with this producer of Les Cras from the same vintage was better. Golden in colour, a little over-ripe and sweet but not as offensive as the Javillier. Weekday quaffer sort of wine that didn’t leave much of an impression. (87 pts.)
  • 2017 Domaine Robert Sirugue Vosne-Romanée - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée
    New-school style in every way possible. Colour was similar to hipster Grenache with a shade of brilliant, ruby red. Nose was quite high-toned and jammy, the palate lacked the elegance of a Vosne so I guessed 2018 new-school Gevrey. Lacked body and tannins. (87 pts.)
  • 2017 Domaine Ghislaine Barthod / Barthod-Noëllat Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru
    Generous leftover two-day old sample. Thought 2017 Gevrey from a good producer. Seductive, earthy nose, had such silky tannins, concentration and transparency that only the best makers can achieve. Surprised at the reveal since I had the impression Barthod wines took forever to come around, but this was a few days old from a more approachable vintage. (94 pts.)

Photo 15-4-23, 2 48 30 PM1600×1200 271 KB

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Thanks for the write-up, Melvin! Interesting your take on the Girardin, since @William_Kelley insists that style has changed. Maybe not quite yet; 2017 too soon? I own some of these, so I am keenly interested. i am also a big fan of that J. Carillon bottling; in my experience, I don’t think the '17’s will ever equal the 14’s, however.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The style change was firmly in evidence by the early 2010s.

Their Narvaux comes from some very nice domaine holdings that make concentrated fruit, and 2017 is quite a ripe vintage, for me something of a modern-day 1992.

The old style, you should remember, was very toasty oak and quite fat, exotic wines. Today, the fruit tones are firmly orchard/citrus, and the oak is much less toasty, a touch of vanilla pod and pastry cream; and the wines have a lot more tension.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Nice, William, thanks–I look forward to opening one!
BTW, what do you think of the '14/'17 comparison in white Burgundy? I haven’t drunk many of the latter, and the ones I have have been good, but not 2014 good.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Very differently styled vintages, but both obviously high quality. I actually prefer 14 Chablis to 14 Côte de Beaune, and vice versa for 2017. But as ever, it comes down to producer: some 2014s are showing quite a bit of evolution as they close in on age ten.

posts.name: David_Bloch
posts.cooked: The 2014 Carillon was so, so good. Only recent bottles have revealed the depth and complexity - holding off drinking my last two.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Yup, loved 'em but drank them up. Also all my 2014 V. Dancer Les Corbins and PYCM St. Aubin La Pucelles. @William_Kelley drinks and tastes much more wine than I do, and has a better palate and more knowledge to begin with, but I loved all those '14’s. (The Dancer and PYCM, especially at the price, were ridiculously good.)

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Interesting comment, William, about some 2014s “showing quite a bit of evolution”. Obviously, I drink within a much more narrow range than you, but I’ve found 2014s materially less “ready” (let alone evolved) than surrounding vintages. Indeed, I felt several whites actually went through a distinct dumb phase, which is the first time in ages that I’ve really noticed that with a WB vintage. Some 2017s appear to me to be in such a phase roundabout now.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m talking about evolution in the sense of wines being advanced/heading towards premox.

People like 2014 for its acidity, and I myself like chiseled, low pH white Burgundy. But sometimes in 2014 that comes at the expense of ripe grapes, which means some wines lack a bit of plenitude and texture, even if they have an acid spine. The keys to maintaining low pHs are in farming techniques on the one hand, and pressing on the other, not simply by picking early.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I’m talking about evolution in the sense of wines being advanced/heading towards premox.

Definitely had some '14s recently, GCs from high end estates, that were more advanced than I expected. Others at the table disagreed and called them well developed, but I thought they were borderline oxidized already. Probably time to check on my case of '14 Bouchard Monty and see how it’s developing.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Andrew, keep in mind the Bouchard is under DIAM. In my experience, it ages nice and slowly. I’ve not had the Montrachet recently, but Chevalier is still very, very young (imho). What GCs have you found “advanced/ready” recently?

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: I have to say that this has not been my experience, but I am by no means talking GC’s. Lots of materiel, densité, texture in the wines I cited above. I drink lots of PYCM from multiple years, but none have really equaled the stuffing of the '14 La Pucelle.
